content.problemStatement:
For any positive integer, we define a digit rotation as either moving the first digit
to the end of the number (left digit rotation), or the last digit to the front of the number (right digit rotation).
For example, the number 12345 could be left digit rotated to 23451, or right digit rotated to 51234.
If there are any leading zeros after digit rotation, they must be removed.
So 10203 could be left digit rotated to 2031, then left digit rotated again to 312.
Given an integer N, determine the largest integer that can result from performing a series of one or more
digit rotations on N.
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains a single integer N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the largest integer that can result from performing a series of one or more digit rotations on N.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each test case, we generate all possible rotations of the number and keep track of the maximum.
The largest rotation of 9 is 9 itself.

solution.approach: The key insight is to generate all possible rotations of the number and keep track of the maximum. This can be achieved by converting the number to a string, rotating the string, and then converting it back to an integer. The time complexity is O(T * N), where T is the number of test cases and N is the number of digits in the number.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N) per test case, O(T * N) total.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — the dominant structure is the string representation of the number.

code.Python3:
import sys
for _ in range(int(input())):
    N = input().strip()
    max_num = 0
    for i in range(len(N)):
        rotated = N[i:] + N[:i]
        num = int(rotated.lstrip('0') or '0')
        if num > max_num: max_num = num
    print(max_num)
